prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 29-year-old Black woman.
 
 My platelet count is low--I've been reading about blood cancers and I'm scared this could be leukemia.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had routine bloodwork and feel fine. I had a bad cold about 2 weeks ago that has resolved. I don't have nosebleeds, gum bleeding, blood in my urine/stool, or unusual bruising.
 - History: No blood thinners or new medications.
 - Vitals: BP 112/70, HR 74
 - Exam: No abnormal exam findings reported.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Platelets: 128 x10^3/uL (150-400) LOW
 - Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (12.0-16.0) normal
 - WBC: 6.8 x10^3/uL (4.5-11.0) normal
 - INR: 1.0 (0.9-1.1) normal
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I understand the worry about leukemia, but your results are actually quite reassuring against that diagnosis. Your platelet count at 128 is only mildly below the reference range. Importantly, your white blood cell count and hemoglobin are both completely normal — in leukemia, you would typically see abnormalities across multiple blood cell lines, not just a mildly low platelet count. A very common cause of a temporary mild drop in platelets is a recent viral infection, which fits perfectly with the cold you had about two weeks ago. This is called post-viral thrombocytopenia and usually resolves on its own within weeks. You have no bleeding symptoms, your vitals are normal, and your exam is normal — all very reassuring signs. Your doctor will likely want to recheck your platelets in a few weeks to confirm they've bounced back to normal.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 92%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I understand the worry about blood cancers, but your results are very reassuring against that. Your platelet count is only mildly low at 128 — just slightly below the 150 cutoff. Importantly, your white blood cell count and hemoglobin are completely normal. In leukemia, you would typically see more dramatic abnormalities across multiple blood cell lines, not an isolated mild dip in platelets. A very common cause of a mildly low platelet count is a recent viral infection — and you had a cold about two weeks ago. Viral illnesses can temporarily suppress platelet production or increase platelet destruction, and counts usually bounce back to normal within a few weeks. You also have no bleeding symptoms and your other clotting test (INR) is normal, which is further reassuring. Your doctor will likely want to recheck your platelets in a few weeks to confirm they recover.

TRIAGE: B

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I understand the worry about blood cancers, but your results are very reassuring against that concern. Your platelet count is only mildly low at 128 — just slightly below the reference range of 150. Importantly, your white blood cell count and hemoglobin are completely normal, which makes leukemia extremely unlikely. Leukemia typically causes multiple abnormal blood counts, not an isolated mild dip in platelets. The most common cause of a mildly low platelet count in a young, healthy person who recently had a viral illness (like your cold) is post-viral suppression — your bone marrow temporarily slows platelet production while fighting the infection, and counts recover on their own within weeks. You have no bleeding symptoms, normal vitals, and a normal exam, all of which are reassuring. Your doctor will likely want to recheck your platelets in a few weeks to confirm they bounce back.

TRIAGE: B
